author: @miinyoo
text:
Exfoliation is not a way to mass produce flawless graphene in arbitrary sizes unless you have nearly perfect source graphite which does not exist. 
The only way graphene production is viable is by construction atom by atom and many have tried but no one has really figured it out yet; far from doing it cheaply and on any sort of scale.

The total amount of graphite that needs to be converted is about 10-15 lbs. Any suggestions are welcome.

author: @bobwatson6726
text: The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) notes: "previously, descriptions such as graphite layers, carbon layers, or carbon sheets have been used for the term graphene...it is incorrect to use for a single layer a term which includes the term graphite, which would imply a three-dimensional structure. The term graphene should be used only when the reactions, structural relations or other properties of individual layers are discussed."

author: @THR33SHOES46and2
text: Robert! not sure if youve heard yet. have you tried using soda water? it will clean without difficult toxic nightmare. i will try to send the post i had seen. its co2 carbonate. you might find it if you google it. i read it and instantly thought of you and your applied tests. im sure your brain will understand better what im ranting about. ill try to send it along somehow.

replies.author: @THR33SHOES46and2
replies.text:
ok it was in phys.org. Fizzy soda water may be the key to manufacturing flat wonder material:graphene. check it out,i hope this somehow helps out. 
peace.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the unfortunate thing about graphene is that there is al to of confusion around it mate - the soda water wasn't used to make the graphene - the graphene was made by chemical vapour deposition onto a copper foil. The soda water was used to delaminate the made graphene sheet from the foil substrate. It is important as normally that delimitation is hard to do and creates a lot of waste but the load method stops that - but again the soda water isn't used to make the initial graphene - but thanks for the heads up

replies.author: @THR33SHOES46and2
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith i had no idea what it was for. just seen something cool involving graphene manufacturing. truthfully, alot of the chemistry terms give me athlete's scalp from going over my head so much. fascinates me to no end anyways. it figures, seems youre a step an a half ahead of the curve. makes it easier to understand any outdated newsflash i dig up. who knows, one day ill find something that will make you scratch your head. have no fear of any patent office footrace. i agree that great ideas belong to the people not just the people that want to sell us the future.

replies.text: I assure you will mate - keep looking

author: @Synonymous_Legion
text: Correct me if I'm wrong, but is graphene considered to be bulletproof? (Please respond politely)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i always try to be polite mate - sometimes i know i am not as polite as i could be - but then i am but human. Bullet proof is not really a defined term mate - it's kind of hard to know what you mean - graphene is also not really a single thing - except in one of the narrowest definitions. So if we take that definition and have a look at the theoretical ideas then graphene has a definable strength which would make you think it is 'bullet proof' - but then we have to make that ideal real and that is a different challenge. I suspect this answer isn't the one you are looking for but it is the best one i can give

author: @voxelmaniam
text:
I was thinking that a less expensive version of your high performance blender might be made using a palm router. They turn at very high speeds, typically variable up to 30,000 RPM, have reasonably good power, and are relatively inexpensive. They would probably tolerate longer run times then your typical kitchen blender as well. Of course if your turning something this fast it will need to be pretty well balanced.

Did I understand you correctly to say that the starting material is graphite?

author: @Berserkerbaboen
text:
I was thinking. If enzymes could make graphene, and follow the string of damaged never cells to one end whyle building graphene in a string or cocoon around it/attacked to it, the nerve cell would be fixed, and also indestructible  by further attacks by the own immune system. 
But can graphene conduct the current needed to make nerve cells work again? The outer layer of nervecells is the stuff that normally conducts current, but is killed by the own immune system. Here is where my graphenemaking enzymes come into play. 
I totally lack the means to find this out. But I know enzymes are 'just coded droids'... :P

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +K Schot I didn't think nerve cells conducted current in the same way a wire does - i though ion exchange across the membrane was responsible for a potential that can travel - you might want to check that

replies.author: @Berserkerbaboen
replies.text: You might be right. But if the replacement conducts current differently, yet still works it might still work. I was just reading about damaged nerve cells, and thought about graphene as a solution. It's in the same size/scale categorie.

replies.author: @Berserkerbaboen
replies.text:
programming an enzyme to follow broken nervecells and build graphene around it is very high-tech. Maybe even Sf still. Maybe we need a (the) superchip to calculate the DNA needed to create these enzymes.
Last thing we want is just tinkering around and the enzymes then just connecting all nerve cells in one big graphene web. Raise a leg=also raise your eyebrow then :D

replies.author: @Digitalman271
replies.text: What if you just blended water and graphite with a steadily positively charged blade or mixer? I would ASSUME the blade/mixer would draw the electrons from the mixture. Sorta like a van de graaff generator. I would also assume the graphite would ionize first due to it's higher conductivity. The effect (hopefully) would keep the carbon atoms separate as long as the charge remains. Would you say such an approach could work?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @davidlarson3905
text:
Hello again Robert! I'm working away on this recipe.
I will list the parameters and results of my first experiment below, along with a question for efficiency.

January 15, 2016
800 max rpm
10:00 a.m. - 2:00 p.m.

450ml Acetone
150ml Deionized Water
25g Graphite - 5 micron
Result:
*500 ml of graphene suspended in solution, 100 ml layer of graphite settled to bottom. Possibly not enough co-solvent for graphite to exfoliate into? Possibly not long enough stir time?*

January 19, 2016 (using same batch as above)
Added 150ml Acetone - 600ml total
Added 50ml DI Water - 200ml total

800 rpm max
5:00 p.m. - 9:00 p.m.
*There is still a layer of about 100ml of graphite settled on the bottom*

Here is my question: Is it possible to exfoliate ALL of the graphite into graphene using this method? Why is there still a bit of graphite that settles to the bottom?

I have a process ready to use to boil off the acetone - water co-solvent, then re-capture it for re-use (distillation basically), then I could claim my graphene in the bottom of the boil pot and have the skeleton for a streamlined, effiecient process that is VERY cost effective.

However, in order for this to work as streamlined and efficiently as I would like, and yield only graphene without risk of pouring off some graphite into the boil pot, I need ALL of the graphite to exfoliate into graphene. 
Is this possible?

Thanks again for all the videos and help. Such an amazing material, and I'm very excited to experiment with it in the near future.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +David Larson it's not possible to exfoliate all in one pass mate - drain off the exfoliated stuff - add more solvent and re exfoliate the remainder - you should see it reduce in volume as it is exfoliated into the solvents

replies.author: @davidlarson3905
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith Hmm okay. As the stubborn Northerner I am, I will experiment on the side to see if there is a way to change that, but for now I will just drain off the top and go from there!

Thanks again!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +David Larson lol - we stubborn northerners get everywhere lol - good luck with it mate

author: @Jason-io2vy
text: That isn't graphene. Unless its one atom thick it doesn't form the strength and zero resistance characteristics of graphene. Which is a function of the one atom thick molecular structure. You can test this my simply checking the resistance or trying to cut it in have. If you don't get zero resistance or your able to cut it, then you do not have graphene you have simple graphite.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Walther WagDoodle please read through some of the comments - this is a discussion that has been had many times - oh and basically - you are both right and wrong

author: @renechauvet1056
text:
Great work!! If you allow the acetone and water to evaporate will you get a graphene powder? I was under the impression the graphene would re-combine into graphite? I would like your thoughts on this guy...(see link). He claims to be making 50g+ of ultra high quality graphene in what looks like dried powdered form.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZZJuYtcWOqo

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think there are three processes - recombining, restacking and agglomoration. Graphene won't really recombine as it is more like a stack of plates in the first place - but it might restack - agglomoration is just the aggregation of particles - all nano particles suffer from a degree of agglomoration - that's why I add a surfactant to this solution which also prevents restacking. I don't really know what he has done to make his graphene so I can't really comment on that sorry

author: @allancorfield279
text:
Very interesting and informative.

Going back 45 yrs to Uni days, we evaluated blenders by blending dry wood shavings and sawdust. A good blender reduced the wood to wood flour, the finer the flour the better the blender. 

 I seem to recall that some Consumer review magazines (CHOICE here in Oz) also used this test in the past. But dodgy recollection may be misleading me,

author: @ZephodBeeblebrox
text:
Aha... A way around using soap and having to use a defoaming agent! I knew you'd do it!

I'm trying to understand what's going on in the video but I must admit that I got a little confused at times.

I'm assuming that the graphite didn't dissolve entirely into the acetone/water mix which is why you're mentioning sedimentation. Would, perhaps pouring it through a coffee filter expedite removal of larger pieces or might it add extra contaminants from the filter?

I'd love to see you using equipment that's available in the average house.

author: @Liberallez
text:
FANTASTIC!! You are here-by awarded the title "People's High Commissioner of Information".
I left a comment on your "new lab" video but I'll ask here (where you may see it more readily).
Does the "graphene ink" maintain the hexagonal matrix, graphene is famous for, when it is applied?
As I mentioned over there, I'm contemplating ideas prompted by Viktor Grebennikov in his work on "cavity structural effect" which involves the "honey comb" structure.
I think your expansive curiosity would have great fun with that and you can do some reading here: http://www.rexresearch.com/grebenn/grebenn.htm
Thanks,
BE

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Interesting stuff - and I read all the posts mate - in fact I don't particularly like multiple postings of the same question - it's the youtube equivalent of nagging - i get some people who post the same question across 5 or 6 videos - i normally delete that kind of activity. You have to realise that high concentrations of graphene are more or less guaranteed to agglomerate and reaggregate unless oyu add something to stop that. So, there is bound to be that happening in the ink - to what degree I have no idea. All I can say is the graphene in the ink is sufficient to give a 8-10 times improvement in conductivity over other conductive inks and that is impressive enough. If it will do the job you want - well again - i don't know - but I would probably try it and see - if it was me

replies.author: @Liberallez
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
Sorry, I didn't mean to nag/spam. I'm just very intrigued and enthusiastic about the information you're sharing and I didn't know how often you went to older posts. The processes I'm contemplating are more concerned with the atomic hexagonal structure than with the electrical conductivity, per se. That's why I wondered if you knew whether that structure (in a single layer of graphene) carries through to thicker clumped/agglomerated "coatings" of the material or whether it is peculiar to a single layer and I would therefore need to maintain greater control to maintain that structure.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: incidentally your previous comment was fine - i just deleted the duplication. I check all posts all the time. the structure is maintained

replies.author: @Liberallez
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Excellent! Thank you. I'll let you know if anything comes of my experiments.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cool

author: @PESCADORSHOES
text:
Hi Bob. Thanks a lot for your wonderful work. 
By the way, I thought that I understood English but is not so easy as the Hollywood films.
So, let me kindly invite your followers to help non english speakers to see your videos adding spanish captions, or any other language of course. I think your work deserves to be translated. 
Regards, 
Joan

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like that a lot as an idea mate - I am not sure how we would do it - but if it could be done I would certainly like to help do it

replies.author: @SuperKoldman
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith  I was going to comment the same thing like PESCADORSHOES about translating. I´m spanish and i can speak/understand english in most of times, but now i know that my level is not good enough for understand more than 40% of what you say in your videos. I use auto translate subtitles, but is not good, most of times is understandig and subtitling soooo bad.
Of course this is not a request for you, who i think is doing already one of the most important things than a human could do in this time and in this world. 
Opening people's mind, teaching them to learn, and to think for themselves and share their experiencies only for the good of helping us each other... 
The world needs more people like you, with an unlimited curiosity
So, my request for helping to translate to spanish (or another languages), is for someone who firstly can, of course, and also be conscient of the importance of his/her work for share this knowledge with LOTS of non-english speaker people. 
My thanks for that another hero when he/she appears
The show must go on Robert! Thank you for all!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: SuperKoldman Thank you for your support mate and i hope someone does help as i have a little too limited time to do it myself in a variety of languages

author: @JonathanFosdickNano
text: Brilliant use of water and acetone :) Cheers mate for the tip!  I agree, cleaning the soap out of the graphene *is* challenging, to say the least!  Acetone evaporates nicely as well, leaving no residue behind.  Treating graphene with soap at two-hundred some odd degrees Celsius under a vacuum was one way to do it, though time consuming.  I would be interested in the paper's title for the paper regarding sonication with the diverse solvents. Again, thanks for the tip.

author: @leonardjanus5538
text:
4mg/ml? I have hear correct?
Can I do it with planetary ball mill? (teflon lined low RPM to avoid smash)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would think so - but a high shear mill would be better - like a mortar/grinder

replies.author: @gilbertomorais8118
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Are you sure that 4mg/ml concentration after one hour is of pure graphene?
I ask because on an acetone/water + sonication mixture paper I saw they got 0.21mg/ml after 4 hours, and on another paper using a kitchen blender they got 0.22 mg/ml after 5 hours using a DMF solvent. So if you really got 4mg/ml after a single hour you simply beat those researchers by an order of magnitude, and much faster!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Gilberto Morais
give it a go and see

replies.author: @gilbertomorais8118
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith I did and it burned my blender like you said lol
Unfortunately it lasted around 30 minutes, but I got a grey homogeneous mixture (except for the precipitate) that has been that way for a week now.

I got a bit from near the surface with a pipet (no centrifugation or filtration yet.. working on it) and dropped it on a bit on a piece of plastic.

Found it pretty interesting to watch as the acetone evaporates: a bunch of floating graphene flakes float toward each other and become visible blobs of graphite again (at least thats what I think it's happening).

I thought of adding a surfactant next time but that would make the graphene an insulator and I would like to use its conductivity.. Do you know if there are any surfactants that aren't insulators?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Gilberto Morais
there's a few on the market triton is supposed to be good

author: @consciousenergies
text:
Thanks for the insight. I got a little frustrated trying to filter soapy graphene myself. I tried just using 99.9% isopropyl but the graphite doesn't exfoliate in that solution. Was thinking about doing an experiment with toluene, but that is kind of nasty stuff. 
Have you researched or had any experience with electrostatic deposition for graphene layering. I read short little paper on it and it was an interesting concept. Just curious on your thoughts about that as a viable option to get  a near "single layer" graphene sheet(s).

replies.author: @planetengineeringofficial8545
replies.text: you making graphene my friend?

replies.author: @consciousenergies
replies.text: Yeah, I have been dabbling with it. Turned out very soapy like he Robert said. Difficult to clean off and use for capacitor tech :)

replies.author: @planetengineeringofficial8545
replies.text: hmmm....some brain food givem to me....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: do you have the title to the paper?

replies.author: @consciousenergies
replies.text:
http://arxiv.org/pdf/0808.1577.pdf?origin=publication_detail
There you go, its using a gaseous medium for the deposition. Might be possible to use a electrolyte if one could charge the graphene particles and deposit them into a negatively charged dielectric that wont bond to the nano particulate graphite.
